questions.question: You are preparing for a cross-country flight of 286 km on a true course of 022°. You will burn 75 LBS/HR of 100LL while cruising at 157 KTAS, and the winds aloft are 230° at 27 knots. How much fuel is required if you want to land with a 45-minute reserve?

You've planned a 334 NM cross-country flight at 8.8 GPH on a true course of 183°. After flying 1.7 hours and with 108 NM remaining in the flight, the wind has shifted from 310° at 28 knots to 220° at 27 knots. How much does your groundspeed decrease?
Reference the previous question. From the point the wind shifted, how much more fuel will you actually burn to get to your destination compared to the original planned amount?
questions.context: Referenced question 9: You've planned a 334 NM cross-country flight at 8.8 GPH on a true course of 183°. After flying 1.7 hours and with 108 NM remaining in the flight, the wind has shifted from 310° at 28 knots to 220° at 27 knots. How much does your groundspeed decrease?

questions.question: On your cross-country flight, you notice that it takes 4 minutes and 37 seconds to fly between two points 9.8 SM apart on a magnetic course of 117°. If you are flying a magnetic heading of 124° at 6,500' MSL in ISA conditions, and your indicated airspeed is 124 knots, what are the winds aloft?
Find the winds aloft given the following conditions: Cruise true airspeed of 60 MPH, a ground speed of 80 knots, and a WCA of 7° right from a true course of 325°.

Given the following, find groundspeed. Outside air temperature = 78°F; [[NEXT_LINE]] True Course = 220°; [[NEXT_LINE]] Pressure altitude = 3,650' MSL; [[NEXT_LINE]] CAS = 87 knots; [[NEXT_LINE]] Winds aloft = 050° at 15 knots.
How much fuel is consumed during the cruise segment to LGA? (Assume 1 gal = 6.75 LBS)
questions.context: Use the following information for questions 36-40: You are in a hold at DEPDY at 10,000' MSL with 5,800 LBS of jet fuel. Your destination is KLGA (21' MSL) on a true course of 052° and 45 NM away. Your alternate is KABE (394' MSL) on a true course of 277° and 72 NM from KLGA. The average winds aloft are 320° at 30 knots. Your average level flight performance is 250 KTAS and 3,600 LBS/hr of fuel burn. Your average descent performance is 1,500 FPM, 250 KTAS, and 1,200 LBS/hr of fuel burn. During a missed approach followed by a diversion you would climb to 15,000' MSL with climb performance of 3,500 FPM, 250 KTAS, and 8,000 LBS/hr of fuel burn.

questions.question: How long does it take to fly from KLGA to KABE?
questions.context: Use the following information for questions 36-40: You are in a hold at DEPDY at 10,000' MSL with 5,800 LBS of jet fuel. Your destination is KLGA (21' MSL) on a true course of 052° and 45 NM away. Your alternate is KABE (394' MSL) on a true course of 277° and 72 NM from KLGA. The average winds aloft are 320° at 30 knots. Your average level flight performance is 250 KTAS and 3,600 LBS/hr of fuel burn. Your average descent performance is 1,500 FPM, 250 KTAS, and 1,200 LBS/hr of fuel burn. During a missed approach followed by a diversion you would climb to 15,000' MSL with climb performance of 3,500 FPM, 250 KTAS, and 8,000 LBS/hr of fuel burn.

questions.question: You are flying a cross-country flight. The winds aloft are rather strong today at 300° at 48 knots. If you were flying at 129 KTAS on a course of 010°, what is your new effective true airspeed?

While obtaining your weather briefing for your first solo flight, you consider wind limitations given by your instructor. You have an endorsement up to 7 knots of crosswind and 3 knots of tailwind. The METAR reports the wind as 060° at 13 knots, and the variation is 12°W. The following runways are available: 10R, 17, 28L. Which runway will be within your limitations?

choices.text: None of the runways, try again tomorrow.

questions.question: The jet stream at FL330 (ISA+10°C) is 120° at 70 knots, whereas at FL290 (ISA+12°C), it is 180° at 105 knots. On a true course of 195° and an indicated 280 knots, how much faster will you arrive at your destination (1,100 SM away) if you cruised at FL330 compared to FL290?

Your aircraft has a maximum tire speed limitation of 150 mph groundspeed. The airport elevation is 2800', the altimeter setting is 30.21, and the temperature is 19°C. If you touchdown at 119 KIAS, what is the maximum tailwind which will allow you to land without exceeding the tire speed limit?

Your flight is 218 nm long on a true course of 197°, and you plan to fly at 122 KTAS while burning 9.8 GPH. If the winds aloft are 220° at 27 kts, how many pounds of fuel do you need to carry to land with a 30-minute reserve?
What wind correction angle is necessary given the following: Winds aloft are 140° at 35 knots, true course is 220°, true airspeed is 88 knots.
While flying downwind at 85 KTAS for Runway 32 (MC of 332°) with winds aloft of 020° at 14 knots, what heading is required to avoid drifting?
The winds are 340° at 30 knots. If you are landing on Runway 31, what is the crosswind component?
The current METAR winds at KBIL are 160° at 14 knots. You are landing on Runway 10R which has a heading of 101°. What is your headwind component with a variation of 11°E?

You are asked to loiter over a field for 30 minutes so your friend can take some pictures of his crops. If the field is located 85 nautical miles away on a 225° course from your airport and you plan a TAS of 92 knots, how much fuel must you carry to accomplish this and land with a 30-minute reserve assuming you burn 7.3 GPH and the winds are from 260° at 14 knots?

On a cross-country flight you decide to figure out what the actual winds aloft are, so you fly due north and notice that you are drifting 5° to the right. You then fly due east and notice that you are drifting 3° to the left. If your ground speed was 95 knots in both cases, what are the winds aloft?

On a VFR cross-country you notice that the actual winds aloft do not seem to match what you planned with. You take some notes and run some calculations obtaining the following information: TAS 154 knots; flying heading 233° you drift 6° left of course and have a ground speed of 145 knots; flying heading 308° you drift 2° right and have a groundspeed of 300 km/hr. What are the actual winds at your altitude?

As the wind howls at the window, you consider the crosswind limitations of your airplane. Alas, you are just a student pilot, and you have been given a crosswind limitation of 7 knots and tailwind limitation of 3 knots. The METAR reports the wind as 130° at 14 knots, and the variation is 7°E. The following runways are available: 17R, 22L, 35C, and 08. Which runway(s) will satisfy your solo requirement?

choices.text: None of the runways, try again tomorrow.

questions.correctChoice: None of the runways, try again tomorrow.

questions.question: The jet stream at FL310 (ISA-15°C) is 120° at 105 knots, whereas at FL270 (ISA-11°C), it is 160° at 143 knots. On a true course of 210° and an indicated airspeed of 450 knots, how much faster will it be to fly 1,817 KM at FL310 instead of FL270?

What should your airspeed indicator read if your aircraft crosses 6 NM in 2.7 minutes while flying a true heading of 312° holding a wind correction angle of 10° right? Wind is 020° at 24 knots. Temperature is ISA at 8,500' MSL.

Your flight will be 93 NM on a true course of 257°. You will burn 13.2 GPH while flying 151 KCAS at an indicated altitude of 5,500'. Winds aloft are 120° at 27 knots, the altimeter setting is 30.17" Hg, and the OAT is 4°C. How many pounds of Avgas are required to complete the flight with a 30-minute reserve?
What is the groundspeed in climb?
questions.context: Use the following information to solve questions 36-40. An aircraft leaves their departure airport (Elev. 1,076') on a true course of 160° for 114 NM to their destination airport (Elev. 661'). Departure winds are 185° at 12 knots; destination winds are calm. Winds aloft at a cruise altitude of 7,500' MSL are 285° at 21 knots. [[TABLE]]

questions.question: What wind correction angle is required in cruise?
questions.context: Use the following information to solve questions 36-40. An aircraft leaves their departure airport (Elev. 1,076') on a true course of 160° for 114 NM to their destination airport (Elev. 661'). Departure winds are 185° at 12 knots; destination winds are calm. Winds aloft at a cruise altitude of 7,500' MSL are 285° at 21 knots. [[TABLE]]

questions.question: The winds at 5,500' are 120° at 27 knots. You are tracking a true course of 330° at a true airspeed of 154 mph. How much do you need to increase or decrease your true airspeed to decrease your wind correction angle by 1°?

You are planning to fly VFR from Small Town Municipal Airport to Small City Regional. The distance is 234 nm on a TC of 324°. You cruise at a TAS of 287 km/hr. The winds aloft are as reported below. [[TABLE]] Which cruising altitude would result in the shortest flight time?

Your GPS shows your magnetic track is 102° while you hold a magnetic heading of 110° and your groundspeed is 166 knots. What are the magnetic winds aloft if your IAS is 164 knots at 6,000 feet with a temperature of +5°C and the altimeter set at 29.92"?
You require 12° of wind correction to maintain your ground track of 357° at 77 KTAS. How much crosswind do you have if winds aloft are 140° at 27 knots?
You are flying a 045° heading at 125 kts and notice you are holding a 6° left wind correction angle. You then turn to 010° but are now drifting left 4°. What are the winds aloft?

While flying visually along a road, you want to check the winds. You know that the road runs exactly 090°/270° true course (4° W variation), and you are flying east. Your compass shows a heading of 081°, and the correction card says "TO FLY 090 STEER 088". If it takes you 49 seconds to travel between mile markers and your airspeed indicator shows 83 kts, what are the true winds aloft? Since you are flying at low altitude, assume IAS=TAS.
The runway heading is 267°. The ATIS reported winds are 230° at 22 kts. What heading must you fly to track the centerline down final if your TAS on final is 156 km/hr?

You are flying a true course of 065°, and you lost the printout of the winds aloft forecast. You are flying a TAS of 134 kts. You initially fly heading 075° and drift 5° right of course. You correct and fly heading 055° and drift 2° left. What are the winds aloft?

Flying a heading of 167° to maintain a course of 183°, you notice a ground speed of 118 knots while your airspeed indicator shows 99 knots. What are the winds aloft? Pressure altitude is 5,500' MSL at a temperature of -8°C.
What Wind Correction Angle must be maintained while on the approach?
questions.context: Use the following information for the remaining 5 questions. The AWOS broadcasts winds 150° at 24 KTS with a temperature of +9°C, Altimeter 29.79" Hg. The fix "NIFAA" is 11.2 nm from the threshold of runway 18. Runway 18 has a magnetic course of 177° with a variation of 5° E and is located at 1,241' MSL. The weather is the same at all altitudes.

questions.question: You are flying parallel to the highway and are displeased that it seems like a truck is keeping up with you. If you are flying due west at 90 KTS and the winds are 310° at 20 KTS who is faster assuming the truck is doing the 65 MPH speed limit?
What will your ground speed be after the turn towards Cascade Fields?
questions.context: Use the following information to answer questions 29 to 34: [[NEXT_LINE]] Due to deteriorating weather conditions, you are forced to file an alternate airport for your final 466 SM leg from Pine Bluffs (82V) to Sand Springs (8U1). [[NEXT_LINE]] You choose Cascade Fields (D55), which is 120° from Pine Bluffs at 272 NM. [[NEXT_LINE]] You plan for 161 KTAS on a 051° true course to Sand Springs and you burn avgas at 14.3 GPH while holding 65.4 total gallons. [[NEXT_LINE]] Winds are 310° at 31 knots. [[DIVIDER]] Referenced question 31: At your time to turn you decide to fly to Cascade Fields. What will be your true course?

questions.question: The winds at 4,500 feet are forecasted to be from 218° at 24 KTS. What ground speed do you plan to achieve if you cruise at 171 KM/HR on a magnetic course of 085°?
Your ground speed is 145 knots on a 075° course. The winds are 135° at 30 KTS. The temperature is 46°F. What is your true airspeed?

Your course over the ground is 320° but you are heading 314°. The OAT is -10°C and your TAS is 178 KTS. Your calculated top of descent is 67 NM ahead and you figure you will get to that point in 19 minutes 31 seconds. What are the winds aloft?
The winds are 330° at 20 knots, you depart due south, and you maintain 148 KTAS. What is your ground speed?

You are called out to do a search. The winds aloft are 240° at 34 knots. The search area is 120° and 45 NM from your home airport and you will fly at 117 knots and 12.5 GPH. Once you arrive at the search area you will throttle back to 7.5 GPH. If you depart at 1700 Zulu, what time will you arrive in the search area?

You are flying your Glasair on a quick cross country flight at 4,500' MSL, where the temperature is 12°C, and your altimeter set to the local setting of 30.15". Cruise speed is an impressive 245 KIAS. The cruise portion of your flight is 79 NM and takes only 16 minutes. Following your GPS course of 052° true, you see that you are flying with a 4° crab to the left. What were the true winds you experienced in cruise?

Ack!! It turns out that the winds aloft are not as forecast. You are attempting to maintain a true course of 330°. Flying a true heading of 337° results in drifting four degrees right of course. Flying a true heading of 322° results in drifting two degrees left of course. Your true airspeed is 147 knots. What are the actual winds aloft?

Your flight was 129 NM on a true course of 178°. You use your GPS to track directly to the destination and fly at 137 KTAS. If your MH is 169° in cruise and the flight takes 62 minutes, what are the winds aloft? [[NEXT_LINE]] Variation=5E.
From the previous question, what is your ground speed to 07FA?
questions.context: Referenced question 12: Cruising from KEYW to MYNN (092° TC, 483 NM away) your ETP to your first overwater alternate is 50 NM from KEYW. From that point you know the course to your alternate of 07FA is 046°. Cruising at 185 KTAS with winds at 284° at 32 KTS, how far from 07FA is your equal time point?

questions.question: You start out by flying a 210° heading but realize you have drifted 5° right of your intended course. To correct you turn to a heading of 190° and hold a 2° right wind correction angle. If your planned true airspeed is 125 KTS, what are the winds aloft?
Using the answer from the previous question, you turn to the next leg of your flight with a course of 140°. How long do you expect it will take to fly this 25 NM leg?
questions.context: Referenced question 29: You start out by flying a 210° heading but realize you have drifted 5° right of your intended course. To correct you turn to a heading of 190° and hold a 2° right wind correction angle. If your planned true airspeed is 125 KTS, what are the winds aloft?

questions.question: Checkpoint G and Checkpoint H lie on a magnetic course of 315°. Flying a magnetic heading of 323°, it takes you 16:45 to fly between them at 122 KTAS. What are the winds aloft if Checkpoint G and Checkpoint H are 78 KM apart?
What is your ground speed across the bay to San Francisco?
questions.context: Referenced question 18: You are traveling between Oakland and San Francisco course of 229° and distance of 9.6 NM. The current wind is 310° at 15 knots and you are cruising at 93 KTAS. At what point is your time to San Francisco the same as returning to Oakland?

questions.question: You are flying a 320° heading but notice you are drifting 5° left of course. To correct you fly heading 270° but that causes you to drift 4° right. If you are maintaining 105 knots what are the winds?
Now that you have that figured out, if your desired true course was 305° what heading should you fly?
questions.context: Referenced question 30: You are flying a 320° heading but notice you are drifting 5° left of course. To correct you fly heading 270° but that causes you to drift 4° right. If you are maintaining 105 knots what are the winds?

questions.question: You start out by flying a 210° heading but realize you have drifted 5° right of your intended course. To correct you turn to a heading of 190° and hold a 2° right wind correction angle. If your planned airspeed is 125 kts what are the winds aloft?

You cross the VOR at 15:17:56. You track the 040 radial outbound, and at 15:20:14 you are at 5 DME. If you are flying at 159 KTAS, and your wind correction angle is 4° right, what are the winds aloft?
The winds aloft forecast for 9000' reads: 2963+04. If you fly at 9000' and turn directly into the wind, what indicated airspeed will bring your ground speed to 0? Altimeter setting is 29.75.
Your B-model Cessna 182 is indicating 147 mph in cruise at 4,500' (ISA). If your handheld GPS shows a ground speed of 124 knots, what headwind component are you experiencing?
Find ground speed under the following conditions: Wind is 070° at 18 knots, True Course is 216°, True Airspeed is 103 knots.
What is the wind correction angle for an aircraft flying a heading of 189° at 129 knots ground speed if winds aloft are 305° at 41 knots and true airspeed is 106 knots?
Wind is 350° at 17 knots. On a 293° course and at 116 KTAS, what is the ground speed?
What would your planned groundspeed be in the above example?
questions.context: Referenced question 34: You are flying a true course of 145 and a planned true airspeed of 112 kts. If the forecast winds aloft are 240@16 what magnetic heading should you fly? The variation is 12 West and the airplane has a +4 deviation error.

questions.question: Based on the previous question, where should the skydiver be positioned relative to the landing target at 9000' AGL? Assume the skydiver drifts with the wind, time is divided equally at each altitude, and wind change is linear between altitudes. Winds aloft are: [[TABLE]]
questions.context: Referenced question 9: An experienced skydiver plans to jump over a sporting event and land centerfield. The jump from 9000' AGL to touchdown is estimated to take 52 seconds. What is the average descent rate in knots?

questions.question: It took you 10 minutes to fly between your checkpoints 14 nm apart flying a heading of 266°. If you had a planned course of 258° and a true airspeed of 105 kts what are the winds aloft?
Flying at 85 KTS, you drift left of course by 11° on a 120° heading. You turn right and fly heading 155°. Now you drift 4° right. What are the winds at your altitude?

You're meeting your friend John for a $100 hamburger at a nearby airport cafe. You depart your home airport at 1735Z and fly a true course of 357° for 83 nm. Your friend departs at 1738Z and flies a true course of 063° for 97 nm. Who will arrive first, and by how long? [[NEXT_LINE]] Your plane: [[NEXT_LINE]] TAS = 119 KTS. [[NEXT_LINE]] Deviation = +3° [[NEXT_LINE]] John's plane: [[NEXT_LINE]] TAS = 136 KTS. [[NEXT_LINE]] Deviation = -1° [[NEXT_LINE]] Wind 040@19 KTS. [[NEXT_LINE]] Variation = 3E°
Find winds aloft: [[NEXT_LINE]] TAS = 128 KTS. [[NEXT_LINE]] TC = 192°. [[NEXT_LINE]] MH = 202°. [[NEXT_LINE]] Variation = 6W [[NEXT_LINE]] You have travelled 42 NM in 17 minutes.

You are flying 135° magnetic heading at 110 KTAS and discover you have drifted 8° right. You correct by flying 100° magnetic heading. Now you are drifting 4° left of your intended course. What are the winds aloft?

You are cruising at 4,500'. The FD forecasts winds as: [[TABLE]] The temperature at 4,500' is -4°C and you are indicating 112 knots. What is your ground speed on a true course of 175°? The local altimeter is 30.15" Hg.

You go out for a quick $100 hamburger flight in your Piper J-3 Cub. Your destination is 23 nm away on a 113° true course. Upon reaching your cruise altitude of 1,500, you visually set your course for your destination, and notice that you have to fly a heading of 087°. What is your effective true airspeed if the Cub cruises at 75 mph TAS. Assume a standard atmosphere.

Use the information from the previous question. After a painstaking 27 minute flight, you finally arrive at your destination and enjoy your reward. Based on the information from the previous question, what are the winds aloft?
questions.context: Referenced question 21: You go out for a quick $100 hamburger flight in your Piper J-3 Cub. Your destination is 23 nm away on a 113° true course. Upon reaching your cruise altitude of 1,500, you visually set your course for your destination, and notice that you have to fly a heading of 087°. What is your effective true airspeed if the Cub cruises at 75 mph TAS. Assume a standard atmosphere.

questions.question: The winds aloft are 265° at 28 KTS. With a true airspeed of 120 MPH, how long will it take you to fly 184 KM on a true course of 015°?
Referencing the question above, what will your true heading be?
questions.context: Referenced question 2: The winds aloft are 265° at 28 KTS. With a true airspeed of 120 MPH, how long will it take you to fly 184 KM on a true course of 015°?

questions.question: While flying a heading of 130° you drift 7° left. To correct you fly due East which causes you to drift 2° left. If you are maintaining 115 KTS what are the winds?
Your TAS is 8,612 FPM. Your TH is 027° and you are drifting 18° to the right. ATC instructs you to turn left at standard rate for 22 seconds. You are now drifting 17° right. What are the winds?
Find ground speed and wind correction angle under the following conditions: Wind 323° at 105 KTS, true course 108°, 402 KTAS.
Find winds aloft. True course = 312°, true heading = 320°, true airspeed = 179 kilometers per hour, ground speed = 103 miles per hour.
What is the effective TAS of an aircraft flying a 176° course with winds from 088° at 35 KTS with a ground speed of 98 KTS?
What heading will he fly in cruise?
questions.context: Use the following information for questions 11-15: Joey plans a flight from KMRN (1270') to KTYS (979') at 8,500' MSL on a true course of 277°. The total distance is 118 NM and he will cruise at 145 MPH TAS and 8.7 GPH with winds aloft 240° at 14 KTS. His average climb performance will be 650 FPM at 92 KTAS and 9.8 GPH with average winds of 215° at 15 KTS. His average descent performance will be 900 FPM at 110 KTAS and 5.2 GPH with average winds of 320° at 8 KTS.

questions.question: It is a beautiful day to finally get that cross country done! Forecast winds at your altitude are 240°@15 KTS and your true course is 163°. If you plan to fly at 115 KTAS what heading should you fly?

A movie stunt pilot intends to intentionally crash an airplane and has carefully engineered the scene. She calculates that as long as her ground speed is less than 45 KTS at the time of the touchdown, she will have a 95% chance of surviving without injury. The TAS of the airplane at the time of the touchdown will be 49 KTS on a heading of 352°. Which of the following wind conditions will meet her requirements?

If you are flying along with a heading of 028° while tracking 032°, it takes you 22 minutes to fly 59.5 nm at an IAS of 127 knots at a pressure altitude of 9,500 feet and an OAT of -17°C, what are the winds aloft?

You are flying a heading of 013° and see a drift of 8° left while maintaining 154 knots over the ground. You then turn to a heading of 148° and slow to 136 knots, noting drift of 6° right. What are the winds aloft?

Find magnetic heading given the following: [[NEXT_LINE]] Wind: 235° at 32 knots [[NEXT_LINE]] Temperature: ISA+8°C [[NEXT_LINE]] Magnetic Course: 007° [[NEXT_LINE]] Altitude: 8,500' MSL [[NEXT_LINE]] CAS: 155 mph [[NEXT_LINE]] Altimeter Setting: 30.58" Hg
Using the answer above what are your winds aloft if your GPS states your groundspeed is 147 MPH and there is a wind correction of 6 degrees to the left and you are tracking 148° course?
questions.context: Referenced question 30: You are cruising at 137 KIAS at 7500' MSL, the pressure is 30.18" HG and ISA-10C. What is your current true airspeed?

questions.question: You are flying a heading of 230° and notice you have drifted 5° right of course. You then turn to a heading of 205° but drift 2° left. If you are flying at a true airspeed of 125 KTS what are the winds aloft?
From the previous question, to avoid this on your next leg you decide to quickly re-plan on the fly. If your next leg is a course of 175° what heading should you fly?
questions.context: Referenced question 14: You are flying a heading of 230° and notice you have drifted 5° right of course. You then turn to a heading of 205° but drift 2° left. If you are flying at a true airspeed of 125 KTS what are the winds aloft?

questions.question: When you initially called 1-800-WX-BRIEF, the gentleman on the line told you that your winds aloft were as follows: 3,000': 350@15, 6,000': 035@32. Flying at a TAS of 154 knots at 5,500', how long will it take you to fly the remaining 75 NM and arrive in Des Moines?
questions.context: Use the following information for questions 25-28. [[DIVIDER]] Referenced question 25: It is a beautiful afternoon and you decide you are going to fly from Cherokee, Iowa to Des Moines, Iowa (TC = 132°) to pick up your three cousins and fly them around to see some farmland. Billy Jo weighs 45.3 kilograms, Sally Sue weighs 54.4 kilograms, and Cletus weighs 81.8 kilograms. For weight and balance purposes, how many pounds combined do your three cousins weigh?

questions.question: Find the magnetic heading to fly an aircraft at 118 KTAS on a magnetic course of 024° with winds depicted on the PFD as 245° at 27 KTS. Variation is 8E.
Given: True airspeed = 450 KTS, wind at FL340 = 252° @148 KTS, true course = 047°, and temperature = ISA+07. What is the ground speed?
Flying heading 328° you drift 5 degrees to the right, you then turn heading 257° and drift 3 degrees right. At 120 KTAS what are your winds?
Assuming the wind was constant during the test flight, what was the wind?
questions.context: You do not know what the correction is from IAS to CAS on a new airplane. You fly at a constant indicated 55 knots on two reciprocal headings, 180 and 360, to collect data. The pressure altitude is 3655 and the OAT is 20°F. The southbound run lasts 67 seconds and covers 1.4 nautical miles. The northbound run lasts 112 seconds and covers the same 1.4 nautical miles. Use this information to answer the next four questions.

questions.question: You fly a heading of 320 and drift 6? right. Then you fly a heading of 260 and drift 3? left. Your TAS is 155 knots. What is the wind direction and speed?
Find cruise ground speed at FL290 if winds aloft are 255° at 141 knots, indicated airspeed is 207 knots, outside air temperature is ISA-8°C, and magnetic course is 093°.
The wind is forecast to be from 225° at 28 knots for your cross country flight. If your true course is 133° and you plan a true airspeed of 144 mph, what is your ground speed?
Find ground speed under the following conditions: Wind 330° at 108 knots, true course of 122°, 324 KTAS, variation 13W.

As you fly along holding a heading of 145° you notice you have drifted 6° right of course. In an attempt to correct this you quickly turn to 120° which results in you drifting 4° back to the left. Having had enough with the guesswork what are the winds aloft assuming you are holding a constant 95 ktas?
Referring to the previous question, what will your true heading be on the way to Grand Bahama International?
questions.context: Referenced question 6: You decide to live on the edge and fly a Cessna 172 from Palm Beach International, Florida to Grand Bahama International, Bahamas. Using the following information, how far from Grand Bahama will you be when it will take just as long to continue to Grand Bahama as it will to turn around and go back to Palm Beach? [[NEXT_LINE]] Distance between airports: 76 nm [[NEXT_LINE]] Pressure Altitude: 7,000' MSL [[NEXT_LINE]] Indicated Airspeed: 116 KIAS [[NEXT_LINE]] OAT: 6°C [[NEXT_LINE]] True Course from Palm Beach: 095° [[NEXT_LINE]] True Wind: 302° at 39 mph [[NEXT_LINE]] Fuel On Board/Fuel Flow: 48 US gallons / 63 lb./hr.

questions.question: While flying around in your Piper Cub at a true airspeed of 78 KTAS, you decide to estimate the winds aloft. While flying a magnetic heading of 232°, you drift 6° to the right. Then while flying a magnetic heading of 127°, you drift 7° to the left. What are your magnetic winds aloft?

Larry and Harry are both flying their identical Mooney M-20 airplanes to a local fly-in and compare their headings and courses to find the actual winds aloft at 6,500' MSL. With the same OAT of -9°C and matching indicated airspeed of 135 mph, Larry is flying a magnetic heading 336° to maintain his magnetic course of 330°. Harry is flying a magnetic heading of 043° to maintain his magnetic course of 045°. What are the winds aloft? Altimeter setting is 29.62" Hg.

If Harry's sister Mary happens to be flying to the same fly-in and gets the winds relayed to her from Larry, what is Mary's TAS in knots if she calculates that it took 30.5 seconds to cover the last section line or mile over the ground? She is flying a magnetic course of 190°.
Find WCA: Wind: 330° at 30 kts, TAS: 114 kts, TC: 015°
Find GS in kts. Wind: 210° at 22 kts, TAS: 97 kts, TC: 177°
Find winds. TC: 200°, TAS: 105 kts, GS: 93 kts, TH: 185°, Variation 8W
Which altitude has the slowest ground speed?
questions.context: Use the following information to answer questions 11 through 15: [[TABLE]]

questions.question: Find the magnetic winds aloft. TC: 308°, TAS: 134 KTS, GS: 156 KTS, TH: 297°, Variation: 8W

On a windy day, two aircraft in vicinity of each other are communicating and wish to find the actual winds aloft. The first aircraft is flying a magnetic heading of 287° and notices a drift of 6° to the right. The second aircraft is flying a magnetic heading of 352° and notices a drift of 4° to the left. If both aircraft are flying at 120 knots true airspeed, what are the magnetic winds aloft?
Which altitude will give you the largest outbound wind correction angle?

questions.question: You're flying FedEx 1348, a Boeing 727 from Billings to Denver at a TAS of 483 knots with winds aloft of 310/78 true. Assuming a variation of 12E, what magnetic heading do you need to fly to maintain a direct course of 244 magnetic?

Your inbound heading to Amarillo is 126 magnetic while holding a course of 121 magnetic with a groundspeed of 531 knots.
